title: 3D INJECTING: How to reduce complications by LAYERING-UP risk avoidance [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: 3D INJECTING： How to reduce complications by LAYERING-UP risk avoidance [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: You picture the length of a needle when you aspirate you're only aspirating one [Music] point welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about a very important concept that i think underpins some of the attitudes that ma…

segments.text: you picture the length of a needle when you aspirate you're only aspirating one [Music] point welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about a very important concept that i think underpins some of the attitudes that make for really safe injectors we're going to take this concept and we're going to apply it to products and aspirating and injecting different parts of the face so make sure that you understand this important way of understanding risk and how it can make you a safe injector so let's dive in so what got you thinking about this oh before i answer that question don't forget to give us a like if you have some faith that you're going to learn something today and it'll also help youtube make deliver our videos to you next time you log in so once again there is this continuing debate around different topics in our sector um and obviously being our sector

there's tons of emotion behind it but actually it's often quite small things but important things because ultimately these things affect patients so i'm personally a very big fan of debates i think so much information comes from when people meet and disagree about something not necessarily for the people involved because they often polarize each other and stick with what they've always believed but there's certainly something in it for the audience you get to see how these ideas clash together and make a judgment about what's the right way to understand things so um there was a debate recently around once again around aspirating like should you aspirate and various people come in and put their points of view across and i noticed that the biggest difference between and sometimes uniting the two people was the idea to think probabilistically versus in a black and white manner so thinking probabilistically is very different from either all way of understanding the world so either aspirating makes you safe or it doesn't make you safe either we do allergy tests on everyone or we never do allergy tests on people and these are kind of this black

and white way of looking at things is what leads to most disagreements because actually i've learned long ago long before i got into aesthetics that often whatever your position the truth is more complex and i'm a big fan of creating that space to have disagreement and nuance so that we can effectively make progress because i think sometimes the black and white way of looking at the world causes a delay in understanding because you people get too pinned to a black and white scenario which is necessarily always incorrect because the world is much more complicated than we can figure out with especially in aesthetics with the tiny studies that we do and the evidence is hard to scrape together so this idea of thinking probabilistically might help people see each other's points of view and i think certainly over time it makes sense to me that it'll help people become safer so make it real for us with the example of aspirating so the aspirating debate centers around it's possible that when you're aspirating if you ever do get a positive

it's probably just a hematoma i've heard people say that and they say that on the basis that there's trauma caused by the injection and even the aspirating itself can cause trauma and then you get a flashback and that's the only reason that you've got a flashback um so that's essentially a black and white argument which is saying that all flashbacks are essentially uh essentially a waste of time they're a red herring and i haven't told you anything um on the other hand there's also this the argument that aspirating doesn't actually work um because well if effectively a lot of fillers don't actually allow product back up the syringe so the probabilistic way of thinking about this is there is there space for this is there space for this technique to chip away at the risk even if it doesn't bring it down to zero because some people do teach aspirating as if it's 100 effective and it's definitely not like you definitely cannot tell with one simple aspiration of the syringe exactly um where you are in the face like you may be right in the middle of a big artery but is there a chance that aspiration will give you

some information which might some of the time give you the ability not to occlude a vessel to try and make this more real if you picture the length of a needle when you aspirate you're only aspirating one point so if you think about the area that you might be in terms of the artery it could only be one percent of that needle that you're actually measuring it's almost it is one percent of the needle that's in the length of the skin that you're actually detecting whether you're in a blood vessel or not so this is one way of saying how absolute is it well it's not it's definitely not scanning the whole face for the position of the arteries you're actually only scanning one point so does that mean you never aspirate well what about the fact that in those potential areas when you have aspirated there's still a chance that you might get blood back so there's still a good reason to test that area because that's also where your fill is going to go at least the first part of your filler now so it's a very important area to screen so it's that kind of nuanced um discussion around yes it's not perfect but it's still

useful i guess the people who don't want who don't like aspirating would say that probabilistically maybe it helps but is that probability does it outweigh the risk of doing an additional intervention so the additional the trauma of aspirating for example yeah i actually haven't seen that come up usually it's more of a discussion around but that's a very good way of thinking about it but usually it's a discussion more around whether it works or not yeah and why do they say it doesn't work so one of the reasons it doesn't work is if your fill is too thick so we did an experiment on this is one of the reasons why i did the experiment which is um this idea that when you're injecting if you've primed the needle it no longer is sensitive to any aspirating or if it is you've got to aspirate for the very minimum 10 seconds which is actually a very long time if you're doing a big procedure your injection takes one second your aspiration takes 10 seconds so it lengthens the time of your procedure enormously if you do that for every injection so people don't like doing it

doing it but um but all of those are uh that they're not probabilistic in the way that they're phrasing it they're saying it just there's no point unless this and actually when we tested it with the fillers that we got a range of different results so many of the fillers very reassuringly aspirate within a second so very quickly and this was in my own forearm we'll post a link in the video and so that you can see that experiment or get the free download which we have the timings of different villas it revealed to me is that every filler has a different probability here of aspirating in a different situation and then if you change the needle that's another different probability and of course different areas of the face have different arteries different anatomy and i don't just mean according to what the textbook says i mean there's differences between individuals we just don't match the textbook huge variation i know there's something like 40 different variations of the facial artery but those are simplified variations the reality is there's almost an infinite variation of the facial artery because little wiggles here and there are not documented as differences and but they are so we have we all have

different anatomy and that means you've different anatomy and that means you've got to think about you've got to think approach the whole thing as in is there a way i can make a tiny incremental difference in the risk of this procedure which makes sense to me and apply that as one of many different steps so it's not this idea that aspiration makes you safe and not aspirating makes you unsafe it's that it's one of many different barriers you put in the way of a potential vascular occlusion not a single one step save you from all the problems because obviously that's just not how it works so we might choose aspirating as one probabilistic way to improve safety other what other choices can we make to gently improve our probability of not causing a problem so actually every angle every filler choice every you know even standing different sides of the bed probably makes a difference in the total risk because um but this only makes sense when you don't think about the individual procedure but you think about

you think about the stack of all the procedures that come out of your clinic over 20 or 30 years because really i sometimes have this image of uh imagine a moving anatomy so you have someone on your bed but actually her anatomy might change every second for the whole time you're with her because that's what a bit what it's like when you change patients every patient has a different anatomy so what you're getting what you're actually injecting is your understanding of the average face but almost like probabilistically so if you look at the position of the archery in probabilistic terms it isn't running in a snake down the side of your face or running in a straight line on either side of your nose it's actually covering a much area a much broader area and then the probability of it being there decreases as you move away from this the highest place of probability but it never gets to zero i mean it liter i mean that's an interesting way of saying it never gets to zero the chance of your facial artery running up the middle of your face like um

well except that you'd see it going through your lip but say the position of where your arteries meant to enter that there's variation enough that from a probabilistic point of view if there were infinite number of patients you could find it anywhere so you can't rely on the anatomy i think that's what dr subio was talking about in one of his lives he says that the anatomy doesn't matter i think the central point there is you it's so much variation that you can't rely on what you know about anatomically but you but uh paradoxically and i'm sure you would have made this kind of point as well you can you must take what you know about anatomy and apply it because that decrease the probability but if you ever decrease the probability but if you ever see two clinicians arguing about the position of the facial artery they're probably thinking non-probabilistically it's black and white it's either here or it's not and the chart the actual probabilistic way of looking at it is that it's almost everywhere it could be anywhere and so we need to think about take a bet where we think it is and then we need to act as if it's everywhere and that's a very different way of thinking and i know it's i know people like black and white things because it gives you certainty but it's just no way

to go through this you'll find yourself to go through this you'll find yourself disagreeing with people all the time yeah i mean i think the problem is less about the disagreement with other colleagues it's about just i'm already feeling a little bit overwhelmed like oh my god there's so many potential problems and i have to think in 3d so i would like the idea of some i would like to decide whether or not to aspirate and then make the decision and that's it you know just like putting your seatbelt on you just decide and it's done and you don't worry about it and you just do it well i am i'm arguing in favor of aspirating even though it doesn't give you 100 protection yeah and maybe even if it only gave you one percent chance of reducing the chance of vascular occlusion there's still an argument to do it and this is where it gets tricky is that um i saw one canadian study where they said it only worked 30 of the time and i've seen people using that as a reason not to aspirate now in my mind i'll have that 30 chance please i'll put it on amongst all the other things i'm doing and um i might even look into that and figure out what affects the probability of a

positive aspiration so one of the things as we already discussed we discovered in our example was that different fillers have different probabilities of giving you a positive and that's only one of the variables because the artery itself will give you a different probability as well on each individual patient and maybe the thickness of their blood all these things contribute to the probability but if i can have five percent i'll have it and um the discussion about the trauma well maybe there's a way it can reduce the chance of trauma being the cause as well by ejecting smoothly and yeah and practicing your technique because aspirating is i have this inkling and i'm open to disagreement on this because there is obviously a lot of science in this area as well but it is tempting not to like aspirating because when you train people like we do if you train someone on their first day of injecting trying to train them to aspirate at the same time is messy like it's they're clumsy they haven't got the dexterity it seems to elongate the day forever that's why we use mannequins to start with um but i've always thought get that get over that hurdle when it's messy and it

might be a bit more traumatic because as soon as you've got your technique right it's actually not dramatic it's smooth it's effective you can see injectors on instagram these days aspirating seamlessly you almost don't notice and so that the argument for trauma goes down when you're good at doing the actual technique and that's easily attainable everyone will do it to be honest even if you don't think about it your brain automatically gets better and better at it so a year of aspirating and you're smooth by the end of it but if you've never done it then it's it looks clunky we did a show on tim's tips for injection smooth injecting et cetera so i'll link that below yeah so tell me about filler choice so this is to illustrate this concept further if you think about the question i often get asked is which is the safest filler now there is no solid answer to that when you think probabilistically because you could look at it as in which is the easiest to dissolve that's one parameter and you may make the argument that a thicker filler is harder to dissolve so if you have a complication it's going to be harder to resolve that complication

but there are other factors as well that affect the total risk of the filler so if you when i looked into blindness and looked at the case studies of people who had blindness after dermal filler and i noticed there seemed to be quite a few low viscosity products in fact some of the first examples of blindness from injections came from very low viscosity things such as a steroid low viscosity things such as a steroid injection and this is an indication i think that one of the risks might be that a product can flow easily through the vasculature so if you particularly with the anastomoses that we have in the forehead so you have an anastomosis between the superficial temporal artery and the super trochlear artery and if you're injecting in the temple one of the cases in the uk was a temple injection that's a very low viscosity product it might flow very easily into the orbit whereas a nice thick product would probably not flow so easily through the vessel and this is just the laws of uh this is purcelli's law in terms of viscosity if you're injecting a thick product through a thin tube it's much harder than a thin product through a thick through the through a thin tube so if you were injected temples which filler would you choose

based on the probabilistic so yeah so i mean it makes sense to use a thicker product from for that one particular risk now you may still cause a vascular occlusion and this is where people also get confused is you can't only think about the frequency of the problem you've also got to think about the severity of the problem and we'll touch on this if we talk about needles and cannula because the frequency of the problem will may go down if you use a thin product that aspirates more effectively so you might actually detect some of the time during a vessel if you only used a very thin product so you're actually decreasing the number of vascular occlusions by using a thin product versus a thick product in that occasion but unfortunately if you're using a thin product and it doesn't aspirate you may inject much deeper into the vasculation then you cause a much more severe problem and that is actually true in many different cases i think there are a number of different examples of where you alter frequency but increase severity i think it might be true with cannulas as well because if you look at the data particularly with blindness about um people who had severe injuries from

injectables there's a disproportionate representation i think of cannulas and it's something i think to do with the technique that most people use particularly if you're running parallel with the dorsal nasal artery doing non-surgical rhinoplasty which is common that um perhaps it is less likely with each insertion that you will find yourself in a vessel with a cannula because they have a rounded tip and the theory is they push vessels out the way and they do i believe they do that but if you do get into a little v-shaped connection where there are only ways to go through the vessel and you push through 25 gauge cannula you absolutely can include a vessel but then you're in you stay in the vessel for much longer so that's the downside with cannulas which is that they are you don't tend to move them particularly in the nose in many different directions so you're in the same place you can aspirate most people don't because i don't believe it works test it actually does work in some products some of the time so you might get a positive and when you inject you're staying in that plane for such a long period of time that you basically put a lot more product into that area so

0.1 mils would be a fairly normal amount to inject for a rhinoplasty and that's easily enough to completely occlude the supratrochlear artery all the way into the orbit and cause blindness so it's less likely to cause a vascular occlusion but if you do it's going to be a bigger vascular occlusion and that's probably the trade-off with cannulas what about people choosing the is it the bd syringes for lip filler does that reduce the risk so the bd syringes we tested um the benefit of the bd syringe is that they've got a shorter needle so that decreases resistance when you aspirate and the ones we tested all aspirated at least as well as what came with the manufacturer's syringe or better so that's positive but there are other risks so you could think you know you're actually really having to trade this off against you know some people say are they less clean because they you know your chain you're messing about with product and squirting some into a different syringe and there's no data on that but there is an additional step and i would say multiplying i think it's possible to do that procedure very cleanly and not increase

the rate of contaminants but think about millions of practitioners all doing that with the additional step absolutely the probability is at some point there'll be some contamination so you the probabilistic way of thinking just leads you to entertain those ideas there isn't it isn't black and white there's an extra step probably over the many procedures that are carried out some are more likely to involve an infection than if you didn't have that step how do we apply this approach to which injection technique we choose the way i think about injection technique is you should be able to correlate the probability of injury going up or down when you understand anatomy really well depending on the on everything that you're injecting so as you go deeper are you getting further away from the vessels or closer to the vessel are you going if you're changing your angle and you think about the probability of keeping on going in that direction are you going closer to vessels or further to vessels and this when you think about it um clearly enough and for enough time

it's it starts to guide your decision making so um if you think about for example a nasolabial fold injection we've done a video on this and injecting this area it's very close to a number of vessels but you can imagine how changing your angle of position will alter your proximity to those vessels now it's not to say that you'll alter them by changing it by one degree that you're going to inject the vessel but you are getting closer to where the vessel tends to be and so the probability goes up but similar in the other direction there's a point where the risk is averaged out and then there's a point where you're starting to get closer to the other vessel so the superior labial artery might be the other vessel you think about um you've also got the infraorbital artery and the facial artery so when you're changing your angle of injection are you thinking about your proximity to those vessels and trying to find a position with your understanding of the anatomy that makes sense in terms of how you inject it sounds like a lot of work and it is something i do think you do have to think quite a lot but once you've done it starts to guide you intuitively because as long as

you've got a good mental model of the face and where the average position of these arteries are you start to it actually feels better in the long run because i think what feel what clinicians pick up sometimes is there's a lack of certainty when they're injecting and although we'll never get to zero just thinking about balancing the different risks will be helpful one of the things i also always say to trainees when i'm with them is try and learn the face in such a way so that as you move your needle in different angles and positions you can you know what the outcome of injecting there is going to be so as in what you're incrementally getting towards so if you're injecting toxin around the eyes for example if you're injecting very superficial you may the outcome may be that you get less effect from your toxin but your bruising risk will be less if you go a little bit deeper what's going to happen there you're going to be touching the surface of the muscle that might be closer to causing a bruise very near the surface so you're going to be more effective that's probably a good place to put it what happens if you keep going go through the muscle so underneath the muscle the fat pad you're

now getting close to the origin of zygomaticus major and you now know at that point you may get away with it but at the same time you've got a higher risk of affecting zygomaticus major and causing an asymmetrical smile you know all the way in you might touch the bone what's that going to do well you're going to be miles from orbicularis oculi probably not going to decrease the smile lines you're very close to zygomatic as major and therefore you're going to waste your product or cause a side effect so that way of trying to weigh up at each point what you're likely to get in terms of probabilities in terms of outcomes um is a very good way of understanding anatomy in the face and you do actually do everything so nasolabial fold injection um if you're too superficial you're gonna see the filler if you're too deep but at the wrong angle you might be close to a vessel but it's that way of asking yourself at each position what's the potential difference between the outcome here compared with an outcome in a different place and it it's obviously a lot of work but you only need to do one injection at a time and actually roger killer said this to me once and i really sunk in which is if you think about it for one injection

there's not that much information to know and he's right because there's more than people think but at the same time there isn't an infinite amount of information there there's a certain amount anatomy and then there's your relationship to that anatomy and once you know that you can become very confident injecting that area and you can tweak it when you learn something new but it's a really clear it's a way of shrinking down the volume of stuff you might feel you need to know is just choose one injection and know it well i think you said to us in the past that you get very inflow and focused in the moment before you inject you you're just thinking about that and nothing else in the world well i actually i'll share with you the difference between when i first started and now in terms of my what's going on in my mind when i first started injecting i was replicating what my first trainer taught me i was thinking about what they did and trying to reproduce it and my brain was actually quite quiet it was more my i felt the sense that my hands were doing stuff but it wasn't really a lot going on in my mind and now

it is the complete opposite now i hardly think about my hand i'm constantly thinking about the anatomy and the position and the variable and watching the patient there's a my brain is extremely busy now but it's a nice feeling because i'm in an arena that i now know well enough to feel confident um have a degree of respect and in terms of safety so i'm never completely relaxed but that's actually one of the best places to be when you're on the edge of order and chaos almost and you're delicately guiding each procedure to get an aesthetic result while also being safe it's actually one of my favorite things to do put the music on thinking heavily lose myself for an hour and a half and then afterwards it's like where did the time go yeah that's flow isn't it yeah but it's busy if you're not if you're not weighing up these different parameters while you're injecting yet then start to think about it start to think instead of replicating the technique how do i improve this individual injection to minimize risk as if it was the injection i had to give everyone i ever meet from now until the

end of time but that's kind of what you're committing to anyway so chip await the risk think about those tiny different differences that over a career will make a difference and it's more entertaining for us more funds more intellectually stimulating and it will ultimately be safer as well so it's a micro focus but with a macro result yeah that's a good way of talking about it so you're it's a it's you're very much in the here and now with how you're injecting and applying all your information but you're doing it as if this is going to be carried out um for the rest of your career it's a bit like um with sun cream i always think that about sun cream what that you know living in the northeast northwest of england wearing sun cream seems a bit mad but actually i'm just putting my seatbelt on every day and just committing to the fact that is going to help me have fewer wrinkles and less sun cancer so yeah actually the seat belt is quite a good example of how people do accept things that are based on probability and not absolutes in normal life but then we don't always do that in everything we do in fact a

lot of the as we've talked about a lot of the debates you see on anything like in covert you know do masks prevent the infection or not you know they don't 100 but they chip away at the risk so that's what we're looking for and seatbelts are one of my favorite examples because most times you know we drive to the corner shop our kids school is a mile away from where we are and the chances of us needing that seat belt are effectively zero um the chance even of being pulled over by the police is effectively zero per trip but if you multiply that behavior out over a lifetime the chance of you needing a seat belt i've certainly needed a seat belt at least once um that's where you get the benefit so you have to put the seat belt on every day in order for it to save you once in a lifetime and that's kind of the same i think with aspirating a lot of the ways that we analyze risk and yes it's usually pointless and guess what some people are probably killed by their seatbelts there are examples of where people can't get out of cars or you know there are all sorts of ways that it could make things worse

but we know that the net effect is good and we do that and know that now i don't think we knew when we first started there wouldn't have been any data and this is um this is what i think a lot of the aspirating argument centers around is it's extremely hard to get the data particularly with aspiration because there's so many nuances between injectors the thing we're looking for an occlusion is actually a very rare thing anyway so you're going to have to do a huge study to find you know a handful of it of occlusions and then compare it with another huge study where they were a different number um the power of that study would need to be enormous so i don't think we'll ever get the kind of the final answer you know double-blinded study of people aspirate who don't who use exactly the same technique on people with the same anatomy it's not going to happen so um i don't think we'll ever have a 100 certainty in that but we can work from first principles and that's really the point of to use the seatbelt analogy if people are not flying through the windscreen screen that might be a good thing and maybe we should keep them in the car

uh similarly if you can get blood back in your syringe some of the time that probably has come from an artery now not all the time and there are lots of other things and some of the time you'll be in an artery and you won't get anything back but does it overall make sense that bit of information on those days could be useful and for me it's cut and dry of course that information could be useful and i'm not saying it's 100 useful though that's the key it's just that it helps it's one more data point to make a decision with but we're talking about probabilities and stuff but i tell you what makes it really real for a human being is when you see on the forums clinicians doing an aspiration getting one a flashback for the first time and then they put the picture up on the forum and they're shaking and they're kind of nervous but also happy because they're like yeah thank god that could have been really bad and it's the same with the car you know when you do when you are in a bit of a bumper bashing or something and it saves you the seat belt you think god thank god yeah bicycle helmet is another example um there are lots of occasions where you

only appreciate it when you've gone through it the only thing with aspirating is it sometimes scares clinicians i always think you should be reassured it's a signal that you've got something back they're usually proud i find on the groups scared but proud yeah um it's reassurance that it does work clinically because obviously the theory makes perfect sense but it's nice to have that reassurance that it actually works in real life at least some of the time so i hope you've understood that today that next time you see people arguing about is aspirating worth doing or not that they're actually both right but in different scenarios and if you can become the clinician who understands the gray area and uses incremental steps in your injection procedure to make you safer then i believe over a lifetime that can only be a good thing for you and your patients but you do have to think differently there is no right and wrong answer there are just little things we can do that chip away the risk and there are many of them so you might have something you're already doing yourself in fact drop them in the comments i quite like this practice for example of compressing nearby arteries when you're injecting

just in case you're in a blood vessel there's less way that it'll flow deeper into that vessel so that's the no evidence behind that makes sense though maybe chips away the probability why not do it so i hope that was interesting to you make sure you like and subscribe and there will be a little link below to the aspirating experiment that i did and hopefully you'll find that interesting tim had his brachial vein injected with different well we aspirated with different fillers it's very exciting experiment if you want the download a link will be below i was pretty brave most of the time [Music]
